coredata.dc:title: Intramolecular hydrogen bond-tuned thermal-responsive carbon dots and their application to abnormal body temperature imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chan
He, Yimin
Huang, Jianfeng
Sui, Laizhi
Ran, Guoxia
Zhu, Han
Song, Qijun
coredata.dc:description: A steric hindrance strategy was used to prepare intramolecular hydrogen bond–controlled thermosensitive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via the solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine respectively with three dihydroxybenzene isomers. The CDs obtained from different isomers have very similar morphology, surfaces, and photophysical properties but exhibited different thermal sensitivities. Meanwhile, the orange-emitting CDs (p-CDs) obtained from o-phenylenediamine and p-hydroquinone exhibited an optimal thermal sensitivity of 1.1%/°C. Comprehensive experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations revealed that even a small difference in substituent locations in the phenyl ring of the precursors can considerably affect the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds and that the CDs with strong intramolecular hydrogen bonds exhibited poor thermosensitivity. The p-CDs were incorporated with reference CDs (B-CDs) that exhibited heating–quenching blue emission through electrostatic self-assembly to construct a dual-emission probe (p-CDs/B-CDs), which exhibited a thermal sensitivity of 2.0%/°C. Test strips based on the p-CDs/B-CDs were prepared to measure temperature fluctuations based on sensitive and instant fluorescence color evolution. Further, this fluorescent colorimetry was successfully applied to a test strip–integrated wearable wristband to measure the body temperature. This study establishes an inherent relationship between precursors and the resulting intramolecular hydrogen bonds for precisely tuning the thermal sensitivity of CDs. It also offers a visual quantitative strategy for the early warning of abnormal body temperatures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Precursor-dependent structure
Intramolecular hydrogen bond
Turn-on thermosensitive carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Fluorescent colorimetry
Abnormal body temperature

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Relationship between the precursor structure and temperature sensitivity of CDs
Early warning of abnormal changes in body temperature using the wearable wristband integrated with dual-emitting CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the synthesis process. (b) Fluorescence spectra of the o-CDs (top), m-CDs (middle), and p-CDs (bottom) solutions during the heating processes.

Structural and surface characterizations of the CDs. (a–c) TEM images; inset: HRTEM images (top) and size distribution (bottom). (d–f) TGA and DTG diagrams; inset: DSC curves. (g) XPS C 1 s spectra, (h) XPS O 1 s spectra, and (i) XPS N 1 s spectra of the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs.
Mass spectra of the (a) o-CDs, (b) m-CDs, and (c) p-CDs. Inset: the optimized geometry of the fluorophore molecules and their calculated HOMO and LUMO energy levels.
(a) 1H NMR spectra of the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs. The corresponding signal changes in the (b) o-CDs, (c) m-CDs, and (d) p-CDs during heating.
Structural alterations in the two fluorophore molecules connected by hydrogen bonds for the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs during the heating process.

Fluorescence spectra of the dual-emission sensor solution measured during (a) heating and (b) cooling. (c) Fitted curve of I 550/I 460 vs temperature. (d) Six cycles of I 550/I 460 variations measured at 15 °C–65 °C. (e) Photos of temperature visualization by the sensor solution (top) and sensor-based test strips (bottom). (f) Visual detection of body temperature by our portable wristband (left) and a thermal infrared imaging device (right).

head.ce:title: Intramolecular hydrogen bond-tuned thermal-responsive carbon dots and their application to abnormal body temperature imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Photoresponsive Molecules and Materials, School of Chemical ＆ Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
International Joint Research Center for Photoresponsive Molecules and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Photoresponsive Molecules and Materials, School of Chemical ＆ Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiation Oncology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Radiation Oncology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inherent relationship between precursors and the resulting intramolecular hydrogen bonds determines the thermal sensitivity of target carbon dots (CDs) that are used to construct dual-emission CDs probes for the visual quantification of temperature based on fluorescence color evolution.

A steric hindrance strategy was used to prepare intramolecular hydrogen bond–controlled thermosensitive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via the solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine respectively with three dihydroxybenzene isomers. The CDs obtained from different isomers have very similar morphology, surfaces, and photophysical properties but exhibited different thermal sensitivities. Meanwhile, the orange-emitting CDs (p-CDs) obtained from o-phenylenediamine and p-hydroquinone exhibited an optimal thermal sensitivity of 1.1%/°C. Comprehensive experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations revealed that even a small difference in substituent locations in the phenyl ring of the precursors can considerably affect the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds and that the CDs with strong intramolecular hydrogen bonds exhibited poor thermosensitivity. The p-CDs were incorporated with reference CDs (B-CDs) that exhibited heating–quenching blue emission through electrostatic self-assembly to construct a dual-emission probe (p-CDs/B-CDs), which exhibited a thermal sensitivity of 2.0%/°C. Test strips based on the p-CDs/B-CDs were prepared to measure temperature fluctuations based on sensitive and instant fluorescence color evolution. Further, this fluorescent colorimetry was successfully applied to a test strip–integrated wearable wristband to measure the body temperature. This study establishes an inherent relationship between precursors and the resulting intramolecular hydrogen bonds for precisely tuning the thermal sensitivity of CDs. It also offers a visual quantitative strategy for the early warning of abnormal body temperatures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials that have attracted increasing research attention in diverse application fields because of their fascinating merits such as chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, high water solubility, and good biocompatibility [1–5]. These special properties basically depend on the chemical structures of CDs, which generally comprise sp2/sp3 hybrid conjugated carbon cores functionalized with abundant surface functional groups [6–8]. The large sp2 domain or effective conjugated length in carbon cores equips CDs with long wavelength emissions [9–11]. Small changes in the functional groups on the CDs surface could cause considerable variations in the original physicochemical properties, including surface charge, solubility, and optical bandgap (E g) [12–14]. The synergistic effects of the surface and core states are also used to elucidate the structure–property relationship [15,16]. Conventionally, CDs are often highly heterogenetic, i.e., they could possess a highly complicated composition/structure [17,18]. The preparation of CDs with well-defined structures (e.g., size, shape, crystallinity, and numerous functional groups and defect locations) remains challenging. Further, the study of the formation process of CDs is also difficult because there is lack of in situ characterization techniques. Therefore, it is still difficult to understand several unusual CDs behaviors.

In our previous work, we discovered that heating can partially dissociate intramolecular hydrogen bonds (IHBs), enhancing the fluorescence intensity of CDs [19]. Unlike most conventional CDs with heat-induced fluorescence diminishment, fluorescent “turn-on” CDs exhibit a high signal-to-noise ratio and anti-interference, thereby facilitating their application in intracellular temperature sensing and cell imaging [20–22]. Although the universality of our method has been demonstrated, the correlation between the IHB properties of turn-on CDs and their temperature responses is still unclear. For example, how can the formation of IHBs be controlled by selecting precursors with special structural features? How do the type, location, and strength of IHBs affect the temperature sensitivity of CDs?

The thermal sensitivity of CDs can be improved by selecting appropriate precursors and synthesis conditions because the spatial arrangement and reaction behavior of precursors considerably influence the chemical structure of CDs [23–25]. Hydrogen bonds can be obtained using precursors such as benzene derivatives that contain hydrogen bond donors and acceptors. Hydrogen bonds can be adopted as a regiospecific and structure-controlling agent because they are among the dominant interactions that decide the physicochemical properties of the interacting molecules [26,27]. In the field of supramolecular chemistry, hydrogen bond strength can be enhanced by integrating several hydrogen bonds into a functional unit of materials, and the specificity of hydrogen bonds can be enhanced by regulating the bonding sites using the steric hindrance effect [23,28,29].

Inspired by these cognitions, we propose a steric hindrance strategy that can be used on the different positions of substituents in the phenyl ring of precursors to effectively regulate the formation and properties of IHBs in CDs. Three CDs samples (denoted as o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs) were prepared via separate solvothermal reactions of o-phenylenediamine with three dihydroxybenzene isomers (namely, catechol, resorcinol, and p-hydroquinone). Interestingly, the CDs obtained from the different isomers had very similar morphologies, surface groups, and photophysical properties but exhibited very different thermal sensitivities. We used comprehensive experimental characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculations to demonstrate that the steric hindrance strategy exerted a strong control on the spatial matching of multiple hydrogen bonds via the OH···O schemes during the synthesis process. Notably, the substituents in the ortho-position tended to form strong hydrogen bonds with well-matched chromophores in the CDs. Thus, the o-CDs exhibited a low temperature sensitivity owing to the hard breaking of hydrogen bonds during heating, while the p-CDs exhibit a high temperature sensitivity owing to the relatively weak hydrogen bonds. This suggests that this study preliminarily established a precursor-regulated thermosensitive mechanism for the CDs. A ratiometric fluorescent probe (p-CDs/B-CDs) was developed by combining the p-CDs with blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs) through electrostatic self-assembly to increase the thermal sensitivity. The intensity ratio of the p-CDs/B-CDs exhibited a highly sensitive response to temperature variations as well as good reproducibility during successive heating and cooling cycles. Our method elicits a visual quantification method for temperature detection based on color variation rather than only brightness. Accordingly, a wearable wristband integrated with the p-CDs/B-CDs was developed and used as an early warning system for abnormal body temperature through fluorescence color evolution.

O-phenylenediamine, catechol, resorcinol, p-hydroquinone, methanol, dichloromethane, and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. ltd (Shanghai). All the experiments were conducted using deionized water from a Millipore Direct-Q system. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The thermosensitive CDs were prepared using a solvothermal method, and o-phenylenediamine and p-hydroquinone were used as carbon precursors for the synthesis procedure. Typically, appropriate amounts of o-phenylenediamine and p-hydroquinone were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF. The mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and the reaction temperature was increased to 180 °C and kept constant for 6 h. After cooling the mixture to 25 °C, a crude product was obtained, and subsequently purified by silica column chromatography using a mixture of dichloromethane and methanol (20 % methanol in dichloromethane) as the eluent. During the elution, the orange fluorescence band moves much slower than that of the blue fluorescence band, thus these two materials can be clearly separated, which can be observed under the irradiation by a portable hand lamp as the light source (Kiwi Photoelectric Co., ltd. Shenzhen, China). In comparison with the weak blue fluorescence of the early effluent, the orange band showed a much brighter fluorescence, therefore, this fraction was collected in small vials, and then freeze-dried to obtain the orange-emitting CDs powder (denoted as p-CDs). A decent yield of ca. 20 % was obtained based on the mass of precursors. For comparison, two other CDs (denoted as o-CDs and m-CDs) were prepared using the same method with o-phenylenediamine/catechol and o-phenylenediamine/resorcinol as the precursors, respectively. The B-CDs were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine and citric acid as the precursors according to a reported solvothermal method [30].

The dual-emission fluorescent probe was fabricated by simply mixing the B-CDs and p-CDs in an aqueous solution. Typically, an amount of 1.0 mg of B-CDs and 1.5 mg of p-CDs were dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water. Thereafter, the mixed solution was stirred at room temperature for 1 h and subsequently dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (1000 molecular weight cutoff) to remove the free molecules. The mixture in the dialysis bag was collected and denoted as B-CDs/p-CDs.

Filter papers were cut into pieces measuring about 2 cm × 5 cm. Thereafter, these pieces were immersed in a B-CDs/p-CDs solution for 1 min, and before being dried in an oven at 60 °C for 30 min. These dip-coating operations were repeated five times. Thereafter, a test strip was used as a support band for the wearable wristband. The sample slot for pasting the test strip was opened in the middle area. Fig. S1 shows the schematic configuration of the wearable wristband.

The PL spectra of the CDs and B-CDs/p-CDs solution at different temperatures were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer. The thermal sensitivity was determined using Equation (1):
(1)
S
=
I T
-
I 0
I 0
T -
T 0
× 100 %
where S is the thermal sensitivity and IT and I 0 represent the fluorescence intensity or intensity ratio at T (°C) and T 0 (°C), respectively.

The wristband’s process of detecting human body temperature is simple. The wristband must be tightly clasped around the wrist to ensure a good fit. Herein, a heating device was used to heat the wrist skin and stimulate abnormal skin conditions, and the temperature changes were simultaneously detected using a commercial thermal infrared imaging device and the wearable wristband.

A JEOL JEM 2100Plus transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to characterize the sample morphology. Ultraviolet–visible (UV − Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900, Japan). The PL spectra, temperature-dependent emission spectra, and absolute quantum yields were measured using an Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence lifetime data were collected via the time-correlated single-photon counting system on an Edinburgh Lifespec II spectrofluorometer. Femtosecond transient absorption (fs-TA) spectroscopy measurements were performed at 400 nm excitation using a setup provided by the Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Science. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed using a Mettler Toledo TGA 1100SF thermogravimetric analyzer under a N2 atmosphere that was heated to 1000 °C at a rate of 10 °C⋅min−1. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were conducted using a Netzsch DSC 204 F1 analyzer at a scan rate of 10 °C⋅min−1 under a N2 atmosphere. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected in the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 using a Nicolet 6700 spectrograph. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using an Axis Supraelectron spectrometer with Al Kα radiation. The 1H-nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker AVANCE III HD spectrometer. Mass spectra were recorded using a Waters MALDI SYNAPT mass spectrometer to identify the structure in low molecular fractions. Zeta potential measurements were conducted using a Malvern Zetasizer NanoZS system.

The structural optimizations of all the CDs were performed using the DFT method with the Gaussian 09 program and the B3LYP/6-311G (d) basis set [31–33]. The corresponding energies of the E g, highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were also calculated.

The physicochemical and functional properties of CDs ubiquitously depend on the synthesis precursors. Although tunable thermosensitive CDs have been reported, their precursor-regulated mechanism has not been examined. This paper presents a steric hindrance strategy for tuning the chemical structure of chromophores in CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, three pairs of precursors, namely, o-phenylenediamine/catechol, o-phenylenediamine/resorcinol, and o-phenylenediamine/p-hydroquinone, were selected as the carbon precursors. These precursor pairs can only be differentiated by the location of two hydroxyl groups attached to the phenyl ring. Three CDs were synthesized using a one-pot solvothermal method and were purified using silica column chromatography. The three CDs exhibited orange fluorescence and enhanced responses to temperature increments (Fig. 1b). The temperature-sensitive behaviors of the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs were maximized after comprehensively optimizing the molar ratio of the two carbon precursors and reaction temperature (Fig. S2 − S7). The optimal thermal sensitivities of the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs in aqueous solutions were calculated as 0.36 %/°C, 0.65 %/°C, and 1.1 %/°C, respectively, using Equation (1). The difference between the thermal sensitivities of the three CDs probably indicates that the different chemical structures of their chromophores depended on the choice of precursors.

The morphologies and surface properties of the CDs were systematically characterized to clarify the effect of the precursors on the structures. The TEM images in Fig. 2 a–c reveal that the three CDs are evenly distributed nanoparticles with average diameters of 3.8 nm (o-CDs), 3.4 nm (m-CDs), and 3.6 nm (p-CDs). Furthermore, the HRTEM images exhibit well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which correspond to the (100) diffraction of the graphitic structure. The thermodynamic behaviors of the CDs were investigated using TGA and DTG analysis, and the three CDs were found to exhibit similar thermal decomposition processes. As shown in Fig. 2d–f, the thermal decomposition began in the range of 100 °C–200 °C, and the weight percentages of the final residue at more than 600 °C were 32.8 %, 78.1 %, and 45.4 % for the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs, respectively. The two degradation peaks at about 200 °C and 280 °C–600 °C belong to the pyrolysis of the functional groups on the surface and the carbon network, respectively. Furthermore, DSC measurements were used to determine the presence of hydrogen bonds (inset of Fig. 2d–f), and the large endothermic transitions before 100 °C are ascribed to the breaking of the IHBs [34–36].

The composition and structure of the CDs were further revealed by the FTIR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S8a, the three CDs exhibited distinct absorption bands at 3200–3600, 1600–1700, 1300–1400, and 1000–1100 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH/OH, CO/CN, CN, and CO bonds, respectively [37–39]. The XPS survey spectra in Fig. S8b show that the CDs comprised C (284.8–284.9 eV), N (399.8–400.0 eV), and O (531.1–531.4 eV). Further, the atomic percentages of these three elements are listed in the inset of Fig. S8b, suggesting that carbon is still the main component of CDs. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2g) can be fitted into three peaks at about 284.4, 285.5, and 286.8 eV, which correspond to sp2/sp3 carbon (CC/CC), carbon bound singly to nitrogen or oxygen (CN or CO), and carbonyl carbon (CO), respectively [40,41]. The O 1 s spectra corroborate well with the C 1 s results, which reveal that the O 1 s bands of CDs contain peaks at 534.2 and 532.6 eV corresponding to O in the COC/OC(O)R and CO bonds, respectively [42,43]. Notably, the equivalent ratio of the oxygenated groups in the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs decreased (Fig. S8b). The relative content of the CO bonds also decreased from 79.8 to 4.5 at.%; however, the CO content increased from 20.2 to 95.5 at.%. The N 1 s spectra for the CDs revealed the existence of pyridinic N (399.1 eV) and amino N (400.5 eV) [5,41,44]. The XPS results are consistent with the FTIR results and indicate that although all the CDs mainly contained the same elements, the relative oxygen and nitrogen contents in the surface functional groups were quite different, implying that they should have different potentials in forming hydrogen bonds.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra show that the three CDs exhibited similar absorption behaviors (Fig. S9a–c). The peaks below 300 nm can be attributed to the π → π* transitions of the CC bonds, while the characteristic absorption bands above 400 nm are probably due to the molecular state transitions [16,45,46]. The fluorescence emission spectra are independent of the excitation wavelength of the three CDs (Fig. S9d–f), suggesting that the CDs had a uniform size distribution and a single PL center. Additionally, the maximum excitation wavelengths of the CDs match their absorption bands. The good comparability of the absorption, excitation, and emission of the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs indicate their structural similarity.

Time-resolved PL (TRPL) measurements were performed on the CDs to unravel the deactivation kinetics of electrons from the excited states to the ground states via radiative and nonradiative pathways. The PL decay curves of the CDs were fitted using a dual-exponential formula (Fig. S10), and the average fluorescence lifetimes of the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs were 1.29, 1.53, and 1.49 ns, respectively (Tables S1). The three CDs contained a short-lived component and a long-lived component, which may be due to the carbon core and molecular states, respectively. The m-CDs and p-CDs had a lower ratio of long-lived components than the o-CDs. The o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs had absolute quantum yields of 2.6 %, 2.1 %, and 3.2 %, respectively. Their relatively low lifetime values and quantum yields indicate that the nonradiative decay processes should be considered. Nevertheless, the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs had temperature-independent lifetimes (Fig. S11) in contrast to most fluorescent materials that can exhibit attenuated fluorescence intensities and lifetimes as the temperature increases [47,48]. This peculiarity was confirmed in our previous work, which revealed that IHBs change the electron cloud density of the conjugated system and the coplanar configuration of the chromophores in CDs [19]. Therefore, the enhanced PL intensity and unchanged fluorescence lifetime are caused by the differences in the chromophore structure before and after heating.

The fs-TA measurements were performed on the CDs to determine the transfer and recombination dynamics of the photoexcited charges. The three-dimensional (3D) fs-TA spectra (Fig. S12a, e, and i) exhibit a negative signal at 480–650 nm and a positive signal at 700–1000 nm, both of which are assigned to the stimulated emission (SE) and excited state absorption (ESA), respectively. The typical time-dependent TA spectra shown in Fig. S12b, f, and j illustrate that the SE and ESA signals gradually decreased as the delay time increased. Fig. S12c, g, and k display the kinetic traces at different wavelengths as functions of the delay time. According to the global analyses and the fitted decay-associated difference spectra (DADS) in Fig. S12d, h, and l, three characteristic decay components can be preliminarily attributed to the corresponding relaxation channels. The hot carriers would release redundant energy into the environment via optical phonon scattering (DADS1) and acoustic phonon scattering (DADS2) [31,49–51]. The remaining components would emit luminescence via electron–hole recombination (DADS3), and the corresponding fitted lifetime for this component is close to the lifetime calculated from the TRPL spectra in Table S1. The CDs had similar photophysical processes.

Based on the analyses of the structural and photophysical properties of the CDs, we preliminarily deduce that the luminescence of CDs is dependent on their molecular state configurations, which are physiochemically associated with carbon cores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between the precursor structure and temperature sensitivity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A joint study was conducted to determine the corresponding composition/structure to ascertain the precursor-induced difference in the fluorophore units of the CDs. First, the number of chemical bonds and the position of functional groups were estimated from the XPS and FTIR analysis results. Second, the mass spectra were used to investigate the fragments in the CDs structure. As shown in Fig. 3 , the three CDs had similar structural fragments, and the peaks with an m/z ratio of 475.33 (i.e., the weight of the C28H17N3O5 molecule) were chosen as the model configurations of the fluorophore units in the CDs. The C28H17N3O5 molecule was predicted to comprise seven to nine conjugated rings functionalized with –NH2, –OH, and –COOH groups, and the rational structure was further verified using DFT calculations (Fig. S13 − S15). Moreover, the E g values of the CDs were estimated from the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra using the Tauc plot method [52,53], and they were used as an index to select the rational fluorophore structure. Using the p-CDs as an example, the E g value (2.18 eV) of the NO.8–6 geometry with eight conjugated rings is in close agreement with the experimental data (2.24 eV; Fig. S15a and c). Subsequently, four typical 8–6 skeleton-based geometries with different substituent positions were investigated (Fig. S15b). The No.8-6c geometry was considered the optimized configuration for the fluorophore unit of the p-CDs when the E g values were compared, and the corresponding HOMO and LUMO estimated by theoretical calculations are shown in the inset of Fig. 3c. Finally, the relatively defined molecular structures of the o-CDs and m-CDs could be deduced in the same manner, as shown in Fig. S13 and S14. The fragments for the fluorophore units can be noticeably observed in the mass spectral trace, and their possible formation processes are depicted in Fig. S16. Notably, the substituents of the fluorophores, particularly the hydroxyl groups, maintained their relative locations from the precursor moieties.

To further confirm the proposed molecular structure of the fluorophore units, 1H NMR measurements were performed on the o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the positions of hydrogen in the amino (6.1–7.1 ppm), aromatic hydroxyl (7.5–9.2 ppm), and carboxyl (10.2–10.5 ppm) groups can be clearly distinguished from the chemical shifts in the 1H NMR spectra (6–10 ppm). Notably, the positions of hydrogen in the aromatic hydroxyl groups in the o-CDs shifted from a low field to a high field as the temperature increased (Fig. 4b), implying that the IHBs assembled the fluorophore units in the o-CDs (i.e., ArOH⋅⋅⋅⋅OAr, where Ar represents an aromatic ring) and underwent partial breaking at increased temperatures. Similarly, the hydrogen bonds formed between ArOH and COOH can be elucidated for the m-CDs and p-CDs, as shown in Fig. 4c and d.

We preliminarily deduced the combination mode of two fluorophore units using the DFT method in conjunction with the 1H NMR and mass spectroscopy characterization analyses (Fig. 5 ), and the bond angles/lengths of the IHBs, as well as the angles of the rings, were marked. The bond angles were both 180° because the double IHBs in the o-CDs were more likely to form in a straight line owing to the ortho-position of the hydroxyl groups. Consequently, the fluorophore molecules became distorted, and the angles of the planar six-membered rings changed. It is evident that the angles of the rings recovered well (120°), and the fluorophore molecules could rotate freely due to the heat-induced partial breaking of the IHBs, forming a good coplanar configuration in the o-CDs, as shown in the side views of the two fluorophore units in Fig. S17. This explains why the fluorescence intensity of the o-CDs increased as the temperature increased, as was confirmed in our previous work [19]. It is possible that the hydroxyl groups in the precursors were partially oxidized to carboxyl groups during the production of the m-CDs and p-CDs, and the IHBs formed by Ar–OH and –COOH were located on different aromatic rings. The two IHBs in the o-CDs had the shortest bond lengths (0.992 and 0.985 Å) among all the IHBs in the three CDs and consequently had the strongest bong energy, suggesting that they are unlikely to crack at increased temperatures. These results further demonstrate that the likelihood of IHB breakdown for CDs has a direct correlation with the fluorescence responses to temperature variations. In other words, CDs with strong IHBs would exhibit poor thermosensitivity. The precursor-dependent IHB properties allowed us to predict the thermosensitivity of the CDs to some extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Early warning of abnormal changes in body temperature using the wearable wristband integrated with dual-emitting CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Normal B-CDs with heat-induced fluorescence quenching were chosen as reference emitter, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra with temperature fluctuations from 25 to 65 °C are shown in Fig. S18. A dual-emitting temperature sensor (p-CDs/B-CDs) was built herein. Fig. S19 shows that the B-CDs and p-CDs had zeta potentials of −5.72 and 13.86 mV, respectively, indicating that the construction of the dual-emitting sensor depended on electrostatic self-assembly. Two distinct but interconnected emissions were observed after optimally selecting the B-CD and p-CD concentrations, and the dual-emitting sensor obtained well-resolved, intensity-comparable dual-emission signals for temperature fluctuations (Fig. 6 a and b). As the temperature increased from 15 to 65 °C, the intensity of the blue fluorescence peak centered at 460 nm (denote as I 460) decreased, while the intensity of the orange fluorescence peak at 550 nm (denote as I 550) increased. The fluorescence signals of the dual-emitting sensor were reversibly responsive within the investigated temperature window. As shown in Fig. 6c, I550/I 460 and the temperature had a good linear relationship, with correlation coefficients of 0.994 and 0.996 in the forward and backward temperature-variation processes, respectively. The dual-emitting sensor provided a high contrast during temperature detection because its thermal sensitivity (2.0 %/°C) was higher than that of the p-CDs. The slight blueshift in the emission peak for the p-CDs may be due to the interaction with B-CDs. The fluorescence switching operation was repeated six times in both the forward and backward temperature modes to explore the reproducibility of the dual-emitting sensor (Fig. 6d). Moreover, the corresponding I 550/I 460 remained unchanged even after the p-CDs/B-CDs were stored at room temperature for 90 days (Fig. S20). These results indicate that the dual-emitting sensor exhibits good sensitivity, stability, accuracy, and reproducibility.

Fig. 6e shows that the fluorescence of the dual-emitting sensor solution gradually changed from blue to purple to pink, then to orange-yellow under a 395 nm UV lamp, and the color evolution is visible to the naked eye. These color variations are coincident with the results from Fig. 6a and 6b. These color variations are coincident with the results from Fig. 6a and 6b. At lower temperatures, the blue fluorescence is stronger, whereas, the blue fluorescence becomes weaker, and the orange fluorescence gradually becomes the main PL contributor. Based on the abovementioned results, we fabricated fluorescence test strips by dip-coating them in the dual-emitting sensor solution. These test strips exhibited noticeable sensitive and consecutive color variations, particularly at ∼ 40 °C, reflecting their ability to visually quantify temperature fluctuations. These color variations of the test strips stem from the fluorescence responses of p-CDs/B-CDs to temperature fluctuations. That is to say that the performance of CDs was retained after adhering to the test strips. Thus, our strategy establishes a corresponding relationship between fluorescence color evolution and temperature. Timely access to body temperature data effectively aids in human health warning and identification in epidemic control situations. Although the temperature at different locations on the human body may differ slightly, wrist skin temperature monitoring has become widely used because it is not invasive, comfortable for users, easy to measure, and stable [54–56]. As shown in Fig. 6f, the fluorescence test strips on the wristband exhibited distinct and discernable color responses to variations in the wrist temperature, and their color evolution tendency is very similar to that in Fig. 6e. A comparison of the results from the infrared thermometer and the wristband demonstrates the visual effect and accuracy of the wearable wristband, suggesting that our device can meet the requirements of early warning for abnormal body temperature.

We propose a steric hindrance strategy for synthesizing turn-on thermosensitive CDs with good thermal sensitivity and describe the inherent relationship between the target CDs and their precursors. Detailed structural and optical characterizations and theoretical calculations revealed that the precursor-dependent structure determined the properties of the IHBs and the resulting CDs, and that the CDs with strong IHBs exhibited poor thermosensitivity. Orange-emitting p-CDs with an optimum thermal sensitivity of 1.1 %/°C were synthesized from o-phenylenediamine and p-hydroquinone by optimizing the synthesis conditions and precursors. B-CDs with heat-induced diminished fluorescence were chosen as the reference emitter, and test strips with ratiometric fluorescence were fabricated by dip-coating in a p-CDs//B-CDs solution, which had an improved thermal sensitivity of 2.0 %/°C. A visual quantitative approach was established based on fluorescence color evolution and temperature fluctuations. Additionally, a portable wristband-integrated test strip was developed for the on-site early warning of abnormal changes in the human body temperature.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Chan Wang reports was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China.

ce:section.ce:para: This work is supported by the Natural National Science Foundation of China (51973083) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP22027). Dr. Chan Wang would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.12.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
